summary:
The Safavid Empire (1501–1736 AD) was the most significant Iranian dynasty of the early modern period, ruling a state centered on modern Iran that established Twelver Shia Islam as the state religion — creating the theological and political divide between Sunni and Shia that still defines much of the Middle East's sectarian geography today. Founded by Shah Ismail I, who conquered Tabriz in 1501 at age 14 and declared himself Shah and champion of Shia Islam, the Safavid state rapidly expanded across the Iranian plateau, imposing Shia conversion on a predominantly Sunni population through a combination of missionary zeal and coercion. This brought immediate and sustained conflict with the Sunni Ottoman Empire: the catastrophic [[event:2514|Battle of Chaldiran]] (1514), where Ottoman firearms and artillery destroyed the Safavid cavalry, established the military limits of Safavid expansion and forced a permanent strategic defensive posture toward the Ottomans.

The empire reached its cultural and political apex under [[event:2515|Shah Abbas I]] (r. 1588–1629), who moved the capital to Isfahan, rebuilt it as one of the world's most magnificent cities (the famous saying: 'Isfahan is half the world'), reformed the military by incorporating Georgian and Armenian slave soldiers (ghulam) who were personally loyal to the Shah rather than tribal chiefs, and opened diplomatic and commercial relations with European powers as a counterweight to the Ottomans. Safavid Iran became a center of Persian literature, philosophy, architecture, and the distinctive Safavid school of painting. After Abbas I, the dynasty declined through weak successors; the Afghan Ghilzai tribe invaded and sacked Isfahan in 1722, effectively ending Safavid rule, though puppet shahs continued until 1736 when the military commander Nader Shah formally dissolved the dynasty.
